meta.title: Frames: Film Photography Notebook App for Analog Shooters
meta.description: Track film camera settings with precision. Organize film rolls, log shots, and embed EXIF metadata into scans. Analog photography notebook app for iPhone & Mac.

company.description: Frames is an iOS and macOS app that helps film photographers log, organize, and track their analogue photography process.

hero.heading_seo: Frames Film Photography App: Notes and Metadata for Analog Photography
hero.heading:
The film photography notebook,
built for analog shooters.
hero.paragraph1: Shooting film is a deliberate, intentional process, but keeping track of your settings shouldn't slow you down. Frames is built specifically for analog photographers who want the precision of digital metadata without sacrificing the tactile joy of film. Whether you're testing new film stocks, perfecting your exposure technique, or simply want to remember what worked on that perfect shot, Frames captures every detail exactly when and where you need it.
hero.paragraph2: Unlike generic note-taking apps or paper notebooks, Frames understands film photography. It knows your gear, speaks your language with proper f-stops and shutter speeds, and organizes everything by roll and frame. When your scans come back, you can embed all your shooting data directly into the image files, creating a complete archive that works seamlessly with Lightroom, Bridge, and every other tool in your workflow. Your analog process gets digital precision, without the compromises.
hero.paragraph3: Thousands of film photographers trust Frames to be their shooting companion, from weekend hobbyists to professional archive builders. No ads, no tracking, no subscriptions to services you don't need. Just a beautifully crafted tool that respects your creative process and keeps your data private and portable. Start your free trial and see why film shooters are ditching their notebooks for good.

hero.alt: Frames film photography app and analog photography app for Mac and iPhone, designed to log film rolls, manage metadata, and archive analog photos.

hero_mobile.alt: Frames film photography app and analog photography app for Mac and iPhone, designed to log film rolls, manage metadata, and archive analog photos.

preview1.heading: Quick Shot Logging
preview1.caption: Log your shots instantly with intuitive dials for shutter speed and aperture. The interface lets you capture exposure settings in seconds without interrupting your creative flow, ensuring every frame's details are preserved for later reference.
preview1.alt: Film camera shot recorder with shutter speed dial and aperture selector for quickly logging exposure settings while shooting analog photography.

preview2.heading: Comprehensive Metadata
preview2.caption: Track every detail that matters to your photography. Record camera gear, film stock, aperture, focal length, shutter speed, ISO, flash settings, GPS location, and custom notes. Build complete records that help you learn and improve with each roll you shoot.
preview2.alt: Complete film metadata tracking fields: camera gear, film stock, aperture, focal length, shutter speed, ISO, flash settings, and GPS location data.

preview3.heading: Film Roll Archive
preview3.caption: Browse your complete film archive with every roll beautifully organized. See roll names, shooting dates, and film stock at a glance. Search, filter, and revisit past sessions to analyze your work, track your progress, and rediscover forgotten frames.
preview3.alt: Film roll archive library displaying organized shooting sessions with roll names, capture dates, and film stock details for easy reference and review.

preview4.heading: Equipment Catalog
preview4.caption: Build a complete digital catalog of your photography gear. Add cameras, lenses, filters, and accessories with detailed specifications. Having your equipment organized means faster shooting prep and consistent metadata across all your film rolls.
preview4.alt: Photography equipment management interface for cataloging and organizing film cameras, lenses, filters, and accessories in your shooting workflow.

preview5.heading: Portable Data Export
preview5.caption: Export your shooting data as portable JSON-based .frames files. Back up your records, share them across devices, or migrate between iPhone and Mac seamlessly. Your data stays accessible and future-proof, giving you complete ownership and control.
preview5.alt: JSON-based .frames file format icon for exporting film photography data, enabling backup, cross-platform sharing, and metadata transfer between devices.

preview6.heading: Universal Compatibility
preview6.caption: Your embedded metadata works everywhere you need it. Apple Finder, Flickr, Google Photos, Adobe Lightroom, Adobe Bridge, and Darktable all recognize and display your film shooting data, creating a unified workflow from analog capture to digital archive.
preview6.alt: Photography software compatibility: Apple Finder, Flickr, Google Photos, Adobe Lightroom, Adobe Bridge, and Darktable supporting embedded film metadata.

reviews.heading: What Users Say About Our Film Photography App

review1.title: Reliable Film Photography App That Transfers Shooting Data
review1.quote: Easy to use, reliable, and fairly priced app that transfers shooting data, including geolocation, into scanned negatives' EXIF. The developer listens to feedback and keeps improving. Excellent!

review2.title: Perfect App for Logging Film Shots and Replacing Notebooks
review2.quote: As a home-developing film photographer, I love how Frames lets me log every shot without the hassle of notebooks. It is the perfect and practical solution. Went Pro and never looked back!

review3.title: Track Exposure and Location Data Easily When Shooting Film
review3.quote: Great for recording exposure and location when shooting film, ideal for analyzing settings or keeping EXIF-style data. The developer fixed a bug within a day. Highly recommended!

review4.title: Frames Keeps Film Photographers Organized and Mindful
review4.quote: Getting back into film, I missed having metadata until I found Frames. It replaces paper notes, helps me stay organized, and keeps me mindful when shooting. A must-have for film photographers!

review5.title: Clean, Simple, and the Best Film Notes App I’ve Used
review5.quote: You can tell the developer uses the app too. Every small detail feels intentional, and the interface is clean and simple. Everything works smoothly. Easily the best film notes app available!

review6.title: Fast, Private, and Feature-Rich Analog Photography App
review6.quote: The best analog photography app I’ve used so far. It is simple, fast, and includes all the features I really need for organizing my work. No ads, no tracking, and privacy is a major plus.

review7.title: Elegant and Well-Crafted App for Managing Film Rolls
review7.quote: The app is simple and does exactly what it should. I love being able to name my current film rolls since I sometimes forget which camera I used. The UI is clean, elegant, and well-crafted.

review8.title: Indispensable for film photography enthusiasts
review8.quote: Essential tool for film photographers. Perfect for adding metadata to scans: aperture, shutter speed, filters, location, film stock. Regular updates, great support. Highly recommended.

review9.title: Indispensable for film photography enthusiasts
review9.quote: This is precisely the tool I wished existed for my film photography workflow. All the essential tracking features are present, the customization is wonderful, and I love that I can add my own film stocks to the database. Absolutely delighted.

details.description1: Designed with feedback from the film community to make data logging clear and enjoyable.
details.description2: Join thousands of photographers bringing order and insight to their film shooting experience with Frames.

meta.title: How to Log and Embed Film Photography Metadata with Frames
meta.description: Learn how to use Frames to log film camera settings, organize your rolls, and embed EXIF metadata into your scanned images across iPhone and Mac.

howto1.heading: 1. Create a new roll
howto1.description: Open Frames on your iPhone and tap the icon in the bottom-right corner to start a new roll. Add your film camera, lenses, and choose your film stock. The ISO is set to box speed by default, but you can adjust it to match your light meter. This is where your film photography workflow begins.

howto2.heading: 2. Log Each Shot as You Go
howto2.description: As you shoot, record camera settings for each frame: aperture, shutter speed, focal length, flash, creative notes, and more. Geolocation, place names, and timestamps are automatically captured to create a precise film photography log. You can use the default slider-based controls or switch to a classic form view via the top-right menu.

howto3.heading: 3. Export your Data
howto3.description: Once your roll is finished and all shots are logged, export the data as a .frames file. Send it to your Mac using AirDrop, email, or any method you prefer. This file contains all your recorded film metadata for later use with your scans.

howto4.heading: 4. Load your Data
howto4.description: After your film is developed and scanned, open the .frames file on your Mac. Simply double-click it or drag it into the Frames app for macOS. Your recorded frames will load instantly, ready to be paired with your scanned photo files.

howto5.heading: 5. Associate Data with Photos
howto5.description: Browse your logged shots using list, grid, or full view, and begin associating each scan with its frame data. JPEG, TIFF, and DNG images are currently supported, with CR2 and NEF coming soon. This step links your analog photos with detailed EXIF metadata, preserving the context of every shot.

howto6.heading: 6. Export your Photos
howto6.description: Once everything is matched, export your updated images. Metadata is embedded directly into each photo file, ensuring it stays visible across devices, editing tools, and platforms like Flickr. Your film photography gets the searchable, organized benefits of digital, without losing its soul.

pricing.heading: Pick a plan and start shooting smarter.
pricing.subtitle: Includes Frames for iPhone and Mac with all features unlocked.

question1.question: What metadata can I log per shot?
question1.answer: Quite a lot. For each frame you can record camera, lens, filters, film stock, frame number, focal length, aperture, shutter speed, exposure compensation, ISO, metering mode, exposure program, focus distance, flash, GPS location, timestamps, a reference image, and custom notes. Everything you'd want to reference when reviewing your developed rolls or comparing shots across different lighting conditions and setups.

question2.question: Which image formats are supported?
question2.answer: On Mac, Frames writes metadata directly into JPEG, JXL, TIFF, and DNG files, so your scanned images carry the full shooting context wherever they go. For proprietary formats not on that list, you can export an XMP sidecar file from the iPhone app and place it in the same folder as your image. Most editing and DAM software will pick it up automatically.

question3.question: Which exposure increments are supported?
question3.answer: Frames covers full-stop, half-stop, and third-stop increments out of the box. You set a start value, an end value, and an increment, and the app shows only the values your specific camera or lens actually supports. This keeps the logging interface clean and relevant to your gear. If your equipment uses non-standard values, you can also define custom sequences manually to match any shutter speed or aperture range.

question4.question: Can I log tripods or other accessories?
question4.answer: Yes. The custom notes field on each frame is open-ended, so you can document any gear or detail that matters to your workflow: tripod model, remote release, light modifiers, or anything else. It is not a dedicated structured field, but it gives you the flexibility to capture exactly what you need without being locked into a fixed set of categories.

question5.question: How do I sync data between iPhone and Mac?
question5.answer: By exporting a .frames file. Use the export menu on iPhone, then share it to your Mac via AirDrop, Messages, or any method you prefer. On Mac, you can import it directly and pick up right where you left off. There is no automatic cloud sync by design. Your data stays on your own devices, under your control, without passing through any server.

question6.question: Does Frames work offline?
question6.answer: Entirely. All data is stored locally on your device, so you can log shots, browse your archive, and manage your gear list without a connection. GPS location tagging works offline too and saves automatically with each frame you record. The only time an internet connection is needed is to check for app updates.

question7.question: What is coming in future updates?
question7.answer: The roadmap is shaped directly by the community. Bug reports, feature requests, and general feedback all feed into what gets prioritized and built next. Upcoming updates will focus on expanding workflow tools, improving the experience across iPhone and Mac, and adding features that photographers have been asking for. If you have something specific in mind, getting in touch is the best way to make sure it gets considered.

question8.question: How can I get in touch?
question8.answer: Through the social accounts linked at the bottom of this page, or directly by email. Questions, bug reports, and feature ideas are all welcome. Frames is built with input from people who actually shoot film, so hearing from users is genuinely useful.

Frames makes managing your analog photography effortless, helping you record not only your shots but also the story behind every roll and frame. Whether you work with 35mm, medium format, or large format film, Frames keeps your workflow organized and ensures your photo metadata stays with your images, even after scanning.

meta.title: Privacy Policy - Frames
meta.description: Read the privacy policy for Frames, the analog photography notebook app. Learn how we handle your data with care on both mobile and desktop.

section1.heading: Introduction
section1.paragraph1: At Frames, your privacy is our priority. This Privacy Policy explains how we handle information across our iOS and macOS apps as well as our website. We are committed to keeping your data secure, transparent, and under your control. No unnecessary tracking or collection occurs, and you always remain in charge of the information you choose to share or export.

section2.heading: Frames for iOS and macOS
section2.paragraph1a: Frames does not collect, store, or share any personal data.
section2.paragraph1b: All information you enter in the app is stored locally on your device and never transmitted to any server or third party.
section2.paragraph2: The app uses Apple’s StoreKit framework to manage subscriptions. This process is handled entirely by Apple, and no personal information is accessible to the developer.
section2.paragraph3: Frames does not include analytics, tracking, or any online services. It makes no network connections except when using the system’s location service (CLLocationManager) to capture GPS coordinates if you choose to record them.
section2.paragraph4: When exporting data, the app may create .frames, .txt, or .gpx files. Sharing these files through the system share menu is entirely under your control. The developer does not access or track exported content.
section2.paragraph5: Frames requests the following system permissions:

line1.title: Camera
line1.description: to take a picture directly from the app.

line2.title: Photos
line2.description: to select reference images from your library.

line3.title: Location (While Using the App)
line3.description: to record your GPS coordinates when capturing frames.

section3.heading: withframes.com Website
section3.paragraph1a: The Frames website is a static site hosted on GitHub Pages.
section3.paragraph1b: It does not use cookies, analytics, or tracking scripts. No personal data is collected or stored.
section3.paragraph2: Cloudflare provides basic, anonymous traffic statistics based on server logs. These logs contain no personally identifiable information and are not used for profiling or marketing.
section3.paragraph3: The site includes outbound links to external websites and social media platforms for sharing content or acknowledging publications. Visiting those sites is optional and subject to their own privacy policies.
section3.paragraph4: No forms, newsletters, or interactive features collect information on this website.

section4.heading: Contacting us
section4.paragraph1: If you have any questions or concerns about this privacy policy, you can reach the developer at:

about.p1: Film photography isn't fading away. It's quietly making a strong return.
about.p2: More photographers are choosing slowness, intention, and the tactile feel of film.
about.p3: But the process stays fragmented, scattered across notebooks, rolls, and digital files.
about.p4: Frames is where both sides finally meet, analog and digital, in one place.

section1.title: Log everything.
section1.summary: Frames is an iOS notebook for film photographers. Log rolls, record settings, and capture the details of each shot as you go, without breaking your rhythm. Everything lives in one place, making it simple to stay consistent, follow your progress over time, and connect your notes to your scans once they come back from the lab.

feature1.title: Living archive
feature1.description: All your rolls, cameras, and lenses sit together in a single view. Browse visually or step into the details of any session with ease. Each roll becomes a permanent record you can return to at any time, helping you understand your habits, sharpen your eye, and see how your shooting evolves across projects.

feature2.title: Log as you shoot
feature2.description: Capture what matters, frame by frame, without losing momentum. Log settings, notes, and context for each shot in just a few taps. Frames keeps things structured without getting in the way, so your attention stays on the light, the moment, and the shot.

feature3.title: Follows your scans
feature3.description: Your records don't stay locked inside the app. Export notes and metadata in formats like XMP, CSV, TXT, and more, ready to travel alongside your scans into any editing or archiving setup. Whether you share, store, or process your images, your film data is always within reach.

section2.title: Bring it back.
section2.summary: Frames for macOS closes the loop between your analog notes and your digital scans. Import your logs, pair them with your images, and write metadata directly into each file without modifying the source files. Everything stays connected, so your archive fills out roll by roll and is always complete and ready for editing or sharing.

feature1.title: Seamless imports
feature1.description: Bring your scans into Frames with a simple drag and drop. Match each image to the frame you logged on iOS, and every note, setting, and detail clicks into place alongside it. The connection between your analog records and your digital files becomes immediate and tangible. One roll or a full archive, the process stays quick and precise.

feature2.title: Embed data permanently
feature2.description: Frames writes your shooting data directly into each image file as EXIF metadata. Film stock, exposure settings, equipment, geolocation, and personal notes all travel inside the file itself, invisible but always there. Your scans carry the full story of every shot, so future edits, sharing, or archiving always starts from a complete picture.

feature3.title: Universally readable
feature3.description: Once written, your metadata is part of the file permanently. Apple Photos, Adobe Lightroom, Google Photos, Capture One, Darkroom, and most image tools can read it natively, surfacing your notes and settings wherever you open a file. No extra steps, no lost context, no data to re-enter, regardless of which platform you work on.

feature4.title: Privacy first
feature4.description: Frames stores everything locally, with no cloud connection involved. Your notes and metadata never leave your device unless you export them yourself. No account required, no background sync, no servers. What you capture stays entirely in your hands, private and secure by design.
feature4.privacy_note2: We never share or sell it under any circumstances.

section3.title: Your way.
section3.summary: Frames fits the way you work. Choose how your rolls are displayed, set aperture and shutter speed sequences for your gear, adjust the recorder layout, and pick the controls that matter most to you. No two photographers shoot the same way, and every part of the app shapes itself around your practice, not the other way around.

feature1.title: Flexible layout
feature1.description: Browse your rolls in gallery or list view, control how much detail each frame row shows, and choose your recorder layout, either a floating toolbar or a fullscreen sheet. Every display option is there to serve how you think, not to impose a structure on you. The interface bends to your habits so the tool stays out of the way and your focus stays on making pictures.

feature2.title: Controls that fit you
feature2.description: Tailor Frames to your gear and the way you shoot. Set shutter speed and aperture sequences in the increments you actually use, so the controls stay clean and relevant. Add your most-used shortcuts to the recorder, whether that's metering mode, flash, exposure compensation, or notes, and log faster without compromise.

highlight1.title: Control bar
highlight1.description: Pin the controls you reach for most, metering, flash, exposure compensation, notes, right to the recorder toolbar where they belong. Less searching, more shooting.

highlight2.title: Equipment
highlight2.description: Set up your cameras, lenses, filters, and film stocks once and for all. Frames keeps them on hand across the app so every new roll starts quickly and accurately.

highlight3.title: Geolocation
highlight3.description: Frames automatically tags the location of each frame as you shoot, even offline, and lets you correct it at any time. Every shot carries its place without any extra effort.

highlight4.title: Aperture
highlight4.description: Set your aperture steps in full, half, or third stop increments, or build a fully custom range. Only the values you actually use appear, keeping the interface clean and precise.

highlight5.title: Shutter speed
highlight5.description: Choose standard shutter speed increments or define your own sequence from scratch. The controls show only what's relevant to your camera and style, nothing more.

highlight6.title: Custom notes
highlight6.description: Go beyond standard fields with notes you define yourself. Jot down light readings, reminders, or anything else about a shot that a preset field wouldn't capture.
